them, invite them, the people don't show up. If they do, it's like one couple. And they want no part of this. And they are out of there as fast as they can get out of there. And you do that with them on Tuesday and on Thursday, and you can't save them. They've just had two miserable defeats in a row. It's over. Here's what everybody will come to. It's a product party. They don't even have to lie. They can call friends and family and say, I just got in, whatever, Amway, Herbalife, Mary Kay, jewelry, whatever, and I'm hosting a home party, would you please come? Yeah, we'll come. And what does everybody do when they come to a home party? They buy something. They come, they know they're going to buy something. So if there's five people there, they sell five boxes of stuff. Nobody even has to be any good. It just happens. And at the end of the night, they made 200 bucks. Boom, small victory. The whole list has happened. They are encouraged and emboldened. You have a chance. Now, you have burnt some prospects and there's a trade off. But overall, I can tell you, from the time period of experience that I had, my way wins. You just can't afford to recruit anybody that only has three friends. That's sort of the deal. They need to have like 50 friends. So you can use five of them up on this and then go back to recruiting. But if you don't engineer that small victory, you've had it. It's like over because nobody has any staying power. So this stuck with me and I've tried to apply it to everything I've done ever since. Okay, I get it. They're all going to try and quit. I don't care what it is. Put a bunch of chiropractors in a room and teach them a new way to get referrals. If you don't somehow get them a quick victory, now some direct sales pros used to fake it. So I do Howard Bonnell, vice president of a world book, which is a cyclopedia company. They used to sell those in the homes too. They used to be in cyclopedias. They used to sell Manolos. $800 set of books. Recruiting all the time because most sales people didn't stick. So Howard said when he started, he rose to vice president of world book and he was a recruiter. He figured out pretty quick if they didn't sell set of books on their first two appointments, they're gone. So in his territory, everybody went to the same couple. They didn't know it and Howard bought the first set of books from every sales guy he recruited. He took them to his shill couple. They did their presentation. The shill couple bought with Howard's money. Person was emboldened and he went out and now ran an appointment and actually made a sale. The odds of him making a second sale went up 20 times by faking the first sale. Howard said it was the cheapest $800 he spent again and again and again because it cost a lot of money to recruit people. You might think that unethical. I don't know, but I can tell you it was necessary. In the vac business, when they mass recruit for the vac cleaner business, it was always your first four sales calls. Had to be with family or close friends, but the pitch to them was I'm not going to try and sell you a vacuum cleaner. I just have to run these four audition appointments and the sales manager, my sales manager has to get a good report from you for me to be able to do this. Now you would go to your aunt Nellie and your mother-in-law and your whoever and you would do your four audition appointments. At the end of each appointment, you have to put aunt Nellie on the phone with the sales manager who now closed the vacuum cleaner sale. That's what he did. Mostly they sat in strip clubs and at the time they had a phone sitting on the bar. Now it would be easier. They closed sales. If you got four recruits this week and they all go run three audition appointments, you got 12 appointments, you're going to sell four vacs. That's the deal. Now everybody's going to quit on Monday and all that, but that's what happened. Now the person who then got emboldened and when it made a second sale, his stick rate in the business was high. This served that purpose. You have to be really sensitive no matter what you do to these things. They need to be able to confuse activity with accomplishment and they need to be able to get a small victory quick and easy. You can't have high hurdles in front of people. The last one we'll do, number 62. I also wanted initially from my amway experience, it's a thing called the lottery effect. It's optimistic bias kind of put on steroids. I'll give you a couple of examples, but I'll give you the amway one first. So people will quit things easily, but they are less willing to quit if there's a chance, if it's in their mind there's a chance that suddenly something is going to happen for them that creates success. So on multi-level, the possibility of that, even if you're completely feckless and you do nothing, but somehow we've got one person underneath you. There's a possibility that person might ignite and if there's one person under them, there's a possibility that that person might ignite. And actually the statistics are it's three levels deep where any activity occurs. So when you get somebody you got to get three levels on an end, but you've created a lottery effect. I know I'm not doing nothing and I know I'm not capable of doing nothing, but Bill might and if I quit and Bill goes on to become the next quadruple triple bypass diamond, I'm going to be kicking my ass for the rest of my life. So I will stay in the lottery. How do I do that? Oh, it's $100 minimum purchase a month. Okay, $100 worth of stuff and that's why the rich rule is it's got to go down the sink because it's easier to stay in the lottery if you're using it up. And then I say to myself, wow, Bill's kind of a loser. It's my buddy. How could he be anything but a loser? But that guy he got might ignite. I'll still be kicking my ass. So I got to stay in at least long enough to see if that guy sets fire. That's the whole lottery effect. And huge numbers of people stay in the game because of the hope that that person three, four, five deep in their vertical line might suddenly blow up. From a corporate standpoint, that's the whole, I just gave you the whole business. Now, so in a lot of B2B situations, licensing packages, what's the lottery effect if there's national advertising? And there's leads being created. Well, I don't want to walk away because I might get a lead next month. I might get a deal next month that I didn't have to produce. So I don't want to disconnect from the lottery. I'll wait one more month. I'll wait one more month. So when you can create a lottery effect, so in the GKIC business, you all members, not just the exhibitors, but a fair number of people here, you're here for right reasons, for because you want to be here and you're here because you and I get along and you're here because you wanted the content and all that. But you're also here because this is a platform, a beehive of activity in which you may connect with somebody who might be useful to you directly. You might meet the person with who you're going to do a deal that leads to a lot of money being made, the person that's going to solve a problem for you, the person that's going to be your best buddy for the rest of your life, et cetera. We have a lottery effect in our business. And people will come to the next event because even if they didn't, they know somebody who did. At the last super conference, at the last summit, they made that connection and oh, everybody made money or one sold his company to the other one or they got married or they something. We have a little lottery effect and we play it up gently, not as overtly as I just explained it obviously, although you could make a case for doing that. But we do have a lottery effect in the business. I put it in from the beginning because again, I spent a bunch of time in the National Speakers Association and after the first three years, the only reason I stayed is this. I didn't even go to the sessions, I just went to the breaks because I was there to shop for deals, for people, for contacts, for relationships. That's what I was there for. And that kept me as a member and it kept me going to events. For years after, I would have stopped purely on the merits of the membership and the content and I am not alone. This is, I'm Dan's not the only one who's ever had this reaction to NSA or to an organization or candidly to us. And we don't really care why you're here. I don't really care. I mean, and in many businesses, you don't care why they're there either, right? So like my friends who run Iron Tribe Fitness, a bunch of people figure out that that's such a high caliber member at an Iron Tribe Fitness Center that that's a really good place to be because if you're the local financial guy, you can do a lot of business by being at Iron Tribe Fitness, right? Well, fine. Who cares? If you're running Iron Tribe Fitness, you need 300 people in an Iron Tribe Fitness Center paying dues every month. What do we care why they're there? As long as you're there and keep coming, fine by us, right? So the lottery effect, it works on everybody. It's just a question of whether you can figure out a way to engineer it into your business. So as promised, we have one last half hour for Q&A, just a couple of quick things. When we end, you have a break, but then you have dinner. When do you have dinner? You have dinner at whenever you have dinner. Six. You have dinner at six. Thank you very much. It is sponsored by Clint Arthur, representation by Clint. What he does is clearly important. He's always got something new to tell and show. I urge you to go and then go out and do whatever you want to do or hang out with each other or whatever. If you are returning tomorrow, there is a separate manual to be picked up at seven o'clock in the morning or thereafter. We are starting at eight, not at 8.30. So please be in the room at eight. And I think that's it, as always. I had a pretty good time. I hope you did too. And I think we accomplished a bunch of stuff for a lot of you. And a lot of you have already registered to return. And be with me again next year for Renegade Millionaire 2.0. Thank you very much. We are above the target that was set. So that's nice. That doesn't mean you still can't do it. But you got to do it now. And Q&A for a half an hour, and then we're done. Thank you. There's a little candy left. All right. So from this morning, the idea of sunken costs. You talked a lot about people who kind of already have those in their mind in terms of instilling that into your audience, like in copy or when you're talking to them. At what point do you think you're potentially crossing a threshold where they're going, oh, crap, I shouldn't be in this business at all. I need to get out. This is horrible. Well, if you want to use a B2B example, it almost doesn't matter with a consumer example. Look, the deeper they're in, the less that happens. So your risk of causing stampede out. I mean, look, it could happen in the personal development seminar business, not what I do. But guys like Canfield and Hanson and Est and anybody that's doing pure personal growth, they routinely deal with alienation of affection losses. Not by doing anything untoward, just by awakening someone to the fact that they are in a really crappy relationship with someone who is completely unsupportive. And occasionally, in Q&A, someone will pose that and what should I do? And the honest one, when I was doing a lot of personal development, I would honestly say, get rid of them. Well, you can get sued for that. Because every once in a while, one of them does. They go home and get rid of them. I've never had one do it like with a bullet, but it does happen. Can't get rid of them any other way. Many other way, we can have a little accident here on the next cruise. I mean, most of those people that fall overboard probably didn't. So it happens, but statistically, not existed. So when people have significant sunken cost, they are significantly invested in a thing, relationship, business, investment. You can point that out and irritate it, poke at it, leverage it with very little risk of any sort of statistically significant exiting. Now, the easier it is to exit something, the more you are likely to stimulate significant exit. But if there is really significant sunken cost, most people are not going to go anywhere. So you can leverage it. Now, your best argument, though, is always reinvention or replacement. So the best like it would be, the best argument is, no, you're not going to get out of law. We're just going to replace your law practice with a much better, different reinvented law practice. Is that? I'm sorry. I went to the bathroom. I came back. I think I have the same question. It was about how to defeat the second cost syndrome. So I think you do sense. Yeah. I mean, look, people, you want it. You want to be selling to people who fundamentally can't leave a bad situation. And so it's all I know how to do. It's what I've done. My entire life, right? I own five pharmacies and I'm 58. I'm 61. I'm 63 years old. It's impractical for me to go anywhere. I'm stuck. And now you get to be the person who has the one answer that changes the dynamic of all that. So it's a good thing. Instead of the stinking bad thing, it has become the biggest one of the biggest successes ever in the fixture relationship, direct response business. I can't think of a name now, but it was a big information for almost five years and it was light as fire. Ellen, anybody know? Thank you. Ellen Criedman. That's absolutely right. And it was the best pitch I've ever seen, which is why it was so successful. And so it's called light as fire and it was for women stuck in bad marriages, right? With husbands who didn't pay attention and yada yada. But here's the great part of the pitch. I mean, it's brilliant. Not only doesn't he have to cooperate with any of this, he doesn't have to know you're doing it, but he will be changed by the process anyway. So what's the big bottleneck, the big problem, with every fixture relationship, everything? Whether it's go to therapy, it's read the books, it's take the course, it's do the exercises together, both parties got to play along, right? Well, nine out of 10 times, she's the one buying that stuff. He ain't gonna play along. She's the one dragging him to therapy. He's not gonna play along, right? Oh, date night. Yeah, I'm going bowling. Yeah, I mean, so this pitch was magical, right? Not only doesn't he need to cooperate, he doesn't even need to know what you're doing. And he will be magically transformed in 90 days or less by you doing this process. I mean, it was brilliant, brilliant, because she took away, they'd already tried a bunch of stuff and it had all failed. She took away the reason it always failed. And she allowed the failure to be entirely blamed on the other person. I mean, it was just brilliant. I watched it in awe. The first time I thought they are good, I just mint money with this and they did. Now, by the way, it doesn't work the other way around. They tried it the other way around. Useless, right? Did not work. But, but you know, look, most people, I mean, the dynamic changes with the passing of time. But I mean, there was a time nobody wanted to get divorced. It's not what that was. But still, nobody really wants to leave a 10 year, 12 year, 50, and the longer they've been in it, the less they want to leave. I mean, there's a lot of late in life divorce now. But it ain't that they want to. They just are living so long that, you know, Jesus, another 20 years, I can't do it. Right? I mean, life expectancy is what's causing that whole problem. But I mean, but well, it's an unintended consequence of medical science. Right? I mean, so when they're in this, and they're in this deep, and there's, you have a white magic solution to change its dynamic, the deeper they're in, the less willing they are to just pack up and leave. Right? The more leverage you've got. Thank you. Dan, in the list for new something new, what can we learn from your ability to have such a long liberty and have it's coming back continuously? Well, so to some extent, so Halbert used to say everybody's only got so many clicks on their dial. Right? And you just keep spinning the damn dot. And it's true and false. Right? So the true part of that is in any, in any sort of subject matter driven business. Right? For the most part, so the principles of any subject, mine, John Duquesne's fitness and anti-aging and longevity in any subject matter, the principles almost never change. Right? So the principles are the principles are the principle and particularly in mind because human nature doesn't change. Human behavior changes a little, but human nature really does a change. Right? So the principles never change. The strategies don't change much. Tactics start to change a lot and tools change a lot. Right? So I'm stuck with sort of a fixed slate of principles. Right? So for example, for next year, see, I can't change the basic principles of Renegade Millionaire to make it new. The most basic principle of which is don't do anything, you see a lot of other people do it. I can't fix that. It is what it is. Right? And it existed long before I ever said it has a truth and it will exist long after I'm gone as a truth. So I can't do much with that. Right? But tactics and tools, tactical applications and tools change a fair amount. We have new tools, a lot of new tools this year that we didn't have three years ago because the pace of tool development is accelerating. We have a lot of tools we didn't have 10 years ago. Right? And therefore, tactical applications change. So you can come back to the same set of principles in subject matter and you can freshen it because of tactical applications and tools. And then you rearrange it and you rewrapper it. And it is my contention you don't do anybody a disservice because different people respond to different things within a subject or subject matter at different times. And it is about their readiness and their life events and their maturation of understanding in an area of subject matter. So I would say that that is like half of it is being able to continually rearrange the old, rearrange, repackage, re-describe, reposition the old. That's half. The other half is now to give myself credit, is output is about input. And so if in a subject genre you are going to be able to be new, you do have to take in a lot of information and ideas in order to freshen yourself. And you need it to be eclectic because a customer who is really interested in subject matter will go deep but they won't go broad. A big mainstream audience will go broad but they won't go deep. So you got to do both. And then you have an, I mean I left as much on the cutting room floor as I brought to the room. So the room in the basement where this was all built, there is as much that wasn't used as there was used, maybe more. It's not that there is a real finite supply. And that is really true of any subject matter category. Third, I guess, would be a truth is that the messenger matters not just the message. Meaning simply put, you need a constituency of people who you resonate with who want to spend time with you. And they will then be to some extent forgiving of some retread of same territory. Because the overall experience is something that they want to do. Right? Yes, you and then we will be done. Okay, thank you. On the list of hacks, from perspective of media, are all these created equal? So video versus copy versus whatever other format or are there relevant? Well, any or all can apply to any media. So if that's the question, there is no media prohibition that suggests that anything on that list can't be done here, here, or here. I mean, there are some practical realities of real estate, space and time. So a billboard that somebody sees for 30 seconds as they drive by in a car, even if they drive by it every day, they still only have 30 seconds to absorb its message. So there is some real limitation of what the hell we can do with a billboard, bumper sticker, real limitation of what we can do with a bumper sticker, 30 second spot on radio, real limitation of what we can do. So the number of things that can be done get limited by time and space. But other than that, no, there's nothing there that you couldn't do in a webcast, a live presentation, a video sales letter, an audio CD, a sales letter, a brochure, catalog copy about a product, etc., etc., etc. The other way to raise your question and to answer your question is is some media better than others? And yes or no? So the media answer is always sort of the tool answer. So a hammer is a really good thing to drive a nail. It's not particularly useful to do bypass surgery unless you have no anesthesiologist present. Generally speaking, you need a really sharp knife, but that really sharp knife is pretty useless for driving a nail into a wall to hang a picture. So it's not that the tool is good or bad, it's just about its application. And so media fits with market and message. So for example, I have a VSL that closes over 30% of everybody who watches it on an arthritis product. It's almost useless despite its extraordinarily high closing average because 72-year-old people don't want to go online and watch a VSL. And the economics of the business don't allow us to direct mail it on a DVD. So it's not, it's a great VSL. It's spectacular, but the media to the market match is no good. Now probably that whole list is in the VSL. Okay? All right. Dang, thank you very much. Have a good dinner. Have a good evening. Those of you here tomorrow, we're getting into
